texts.orig: An antioxidative composite membrane with the carboxylate group as a fixed carrier for CO2 separation from flue gas †
texts.text: An antioxidative composite membrane with the carboxylate group as a fixed carrier for CO2 separation from flue gas †

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mingming Wang, Zhi Wang, * Jixiao Wang, Yaqun Zhu and Shichang Wang
texts.text: Mingming Wang, Zhi Wang, * Jixiao Wang, Yaqun Zhu and Shichang Wang

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Received 7th June 2011, Accepted 25th July 2011
texts.text: Received 7th June 2011, Accepted 25th July 2011

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A novel composite membrane merely with the carboxylate group as a fixed carrier for CO2 separation from oxidant-containing flue gases was developed. The obtained membrane shows excellent permselectivity and antioxidizability.
texts.text: A novel composite membrane merely with the carboxylate group as a fixed carrier for CO2 separation from oxidant-containing flue gases was developed. The obtained membrane shows excellent permselectivity and antioxidizability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As a typical greenhouse gas, CO2 content increase in the atmosphere might induce global warming, 1,2 which is the main reason for many disastrous climate accidents and biological extinction. Thus, separation and capture of CO2 has been paid much attention in recent years. 2-6 Untill now, a variety of approaches, such as absorption, adsorption and membrane separation, have been proposed to separate and capture CO2. 7-11 Membrane technology has attracted increasing attention due to its high-efficiency, adaptability, operational simplicity, low energy consumption and investment, and environmental friendliness. 11-14 Among this, the fixed carrier membranes, which could simultaneously improve CO2 permeance and selectivity through the reversible reactions between reactive carriers in the membrane and CO2, have been intensively investigated and made good progress in recent years. However, most of the fixed carrier membranes contain amine groups as carriers, 15-20 which can be oxidized by the oxidant gases (mainly O2) contained in the flue gas. And ultimately the separation performance of the membrane will deteriorate. 21-23
texts.text: As a typical greenhouse gas, CO2 content increase in the atmosphere might induce global warming, 1,2 which is the main reason for many disastrous climate accidents and biological extinction. Thus, separation and capture of CO2 has been paid much attention in recent years. 2-6 Untill now, a variety of approaches, such as absorption, adsorption and membrane separation, have been proposed to separate and capture CO2. 7-11 Membrane technology has attracted increasing attention due to its high-efficiency, adaptability, operational simplicity, low energy consumption and investment, and environmental friendliness. 11-14 Among this, the fixed carrier membranes, which could simultaneously improve CO2 permeance and selectivity through the reversible reactions between reactive carriers in the membrane and CO2, have been intensively investigated and made good progress in recent years. However, most of the fixed carrier membranes contain amine groups as carriers, 15-20 which can be oxidized by the oxidant gases (mainly O2) contained in the flue gas. And ultimately the separation performance of the membrane will deteriorate. 21-23

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Broader context
texts.text: Broader context

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane technology for CO2 separation and capture has attracted increasing attention due to its high efficiency, adaptability, operational simplicity, low energy consumption, low investment cost, and environmental friendliness. The fixed carrier membrane is very promising because it can simultaneously improve CO2 permeance and selectivity via the reversible reactions between the reactive carriers in the membrane and CO2. The reported fixed carrier membrane normally contains amine groups as carriers. However, this kind of membrane will degrade, which is due to the reaction between amine groups of the membrane and the oxidant ( e.g. , oxygen) in the flue gas. Here we present a novel composite membrane with the carboxylate group as a fixed carrier with excellent antioxidizability. This membrane has a great potential for practical applications in CO2 separation from oxidants-containing flue gases.
texts.text: Membrane technology for CO2 separation and capture has attracted increasing attention due to its high efficiency, adaptability, operational simplicity, low energy consumption, low investment cost, and environmental friendliness. The fixed carrier membrane is very promising because it can simultaneously improve CO2 permeance and selectivity via the reversible reactions between the reactive carriers in the membrane and CO2. The reported fixed carrier membrane normally contains amine groups as carriers. However, this kind of membrane will degrade, which is due to the reaction between amine groups of the membrane and the oxidant ( e.g. , oxygen) in the flue gas. Here we present a novel composite membrane with the carboxylate group as a fixed carrier with excellent antioxidizability. This membrane has a great potential for practical applications in CO2 separation from oxidants-containing flue gases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The carboxylate group is difficult to be oxidized by the oxidant such as O2. In addition, the carboxylate group can be hydrolyzed, and then enhance the the solubility of CO2 in the membrane. Consequently, it could facilitate CO2 transport through the membrane. Moreover, the polymers containing the carboxylate group often have good hygroscopicity, which is extremely beneficial for CO2 permeation through the membranes since water is critical to the facilitated transport process. 24-27 Membranes containing both carboxylate groups and amine groups as carriers have been developed in our previous work. 28-30 However, amine groups in the membranes are apt to being oxidized, which will lead to the degradation of the membrane performance. The membrane merely with the carboxylate group as the carrier for CO2 separation has not been studied in the reported literature. In this work, we developed an antioxidative membrane merely with the carboxylate group as a fixed carrier. Sodium acrylate (AAS) and acrylamide (AAm) were chosen as monomers to synthesize poly(sodium acrylateco -acrylamide) (P(AASco -AAm)) with a mass ratio of 3 : 2 (AAS:AAm) through free radical polymerization, as carboxylate groups of AAS can react reversibly with CO2 to facilitate CO 2 transport in the membrane and AAm could undermine the tacticity of the polymer to decrease crystallinity.
texts.text: The carboxylate group is difficult to be oxidized by the oxidant such as O2. In addition, the carboxylate group can be hydrolyzed, and then enhance the the solubility of CO2 in the membrane. Consequently, it could facilitate CO2 transport through the membrane. Moreover, the polymers containing the carboxylate group often have good hygroscopicity, which is extremely beneficial for CO2 permeation through the membranes since water is critical to the facilitated transport process. 24-27 Membranes containing both carboxylate groups and amine groups as carriers have been developed in our previous work. 28-30 However, amine groups in the membranes are apt to being oxidized, which will lead to the degradation of the membrane performance. The membrane merely with the carboxylate group as the carrier for CO2 separation has not been studied in the reported literature. In this work, we developed an antioxidative membrane merely with the carboxylate group as a fixed carrier. Sodium acrylate (AAS) and acrylamide (AAm) were chosen as monomers to synthesize poly(sodium acrylateco -acrylamide) (P(AASco -AAm)) with a mass ratio of 3 : 2 (AAS:AAm) through free radical polymerization, as carboxylate groups of AAS can react reversibly with CO2 to facilitate CO 2 transport in the membrane and AAm could undermine the tacticity of the polymer to decrease crystallinity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The P(AASco -AAm) was characterizd by attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectroscopy (FTS-6000, Bio-Rad of America), and the spectrum is shown in Fig. 1. The absorption bands at 1550 and 1400 cm � 1 are assigned to the C-O stretching vibration of the carboxylate. 30 In addition, the spectrum shows several characteristic absorption bands of the amide. The absorption bands at 3332 and 3181 cm � 1 are assigned to N-H stretching vibrations, the absorption band at 1650 cm � 1 is assigned to

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The composite membranes were prepared by casting a 0.5 wt% P (AASco -AAm) aqueous solution on the polysulfone (PS) ultrafiltration membranes (with an average cut-off molecular weight of 6000 and purchased from Vontron Technology Co., Ltd. (China)) with a pre-set wet coating thickness (the gap between substrate and coating knife) by a coating applicator. After casting, the membrane was dried in a climate chamber (Climacell 222R, Germany) at 30  C and 40% relative humidity for at least 24 h. Thus, the carboxylate groupcontaining active layer with gas permselectivity and antioxidizability was formed on the PS support membrane. The surface and crosssection of the P(AASco -AAm) membrane with 250 m mwet coating thickness were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nova Nano 430, FEI of USA), as shown in Fig. 2. It is observed that the surface of the composite membrane is dense, smooth and defect-free, and the thickness of the active layer is about 257  6 nm.
texts.text: The composite membranes were prepared by casting a 0.5 wt% P (AASco -AAm) aqueous solution on the polysulfone (PS) ultrafiltration membranes (with an average cut-off molecular weight of 6000 and purchased from Vontron Technology Co., Ltd. (China)) with a pre-set wet coating thickness (the gap between substrate and coating knife) by a coating applicator. After casting, the membrane was dried in a climate chamber (Climacell 222R, Germany) at 30  C and 40% relative humidity for at least 24 h. Thus, the carboxylate groupcontaining active layer with gas permselectivity and antioxidizability was formed on the PS support membrane. The surface and crosssection of the P(AASco -AAm) membrane with 250 m mwet coating thickness were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nova Nano 430, FEI of USA), as shown in Fig. 2. It is observed that the surface of the composite membrane is dense, smooth and defect-free, and the thickness of the active layer is about 257  6 nm.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As metioned above, the carboxylate group can react reversibly with CO2 to promote CO2 transport, which results in a facilitated transport phenomenon. In this work, we have experimentally confirmed the reversible reaction between CO2 and the membrane by absorbing and desorbing CO2, which had been employed in our previous work. 17,28 Before ATR-FTIR measurement, each membrane absorbing gas was prepared by placing it in the pure gas or gas containing steam for several hours to reach sorption equilibrium. To desorb CO2, the membrane having reached sorption equilibrium was placed in the air for several hours. ATR-FTIR spectra of P(AASco -AAm)/PS membranes absorbing CO2, N2 and desorbing CO2 are shown in Fig. 3. The dry composite membrane (A), the humidified compostie membrane absorbing N2 (B) and the dry composite membrane absorbing CO2 (C) show quite similar spectra, indicating that N2 cannot react with the polymer, and without water CO2 can not react with the polymer either. The humidified composite membrane absorbing CO2 (D) shows several new bands. The bands at 831 and 1012 cm � 1 are assigned to the characteristic absorption bands of HCO3 � , the band at 1150 cm � 1 is assigned to the C-O (-COOH) stretching vibration, and the bands at 1480 and 1240 cm � 1 are the coupling of the O-H bending vibration and C-O(-COOH) stretching vibration, 30,31 which indicate that CO2 reacts with -COO � of P(AASco -AAm) in the presence of water, producing HCO3 �
texts.text: As metioned above, the carboxylate group can react reversibly with CO2 to promote CO2 transport, which results in a facilitated transport phenomenon. In this work, we have experimentally confirmed the reversible reaction between CO2 and the membrane by absorbing and desorbing CO2, which had been employed in our previous work. 17,28 Before ATR-FTIR measurement, each membrane absorbing gas was prepared by placing it in the pure gas or gas containing steam for several hours to reach sorption equilibrium. To desorb CO2, the membrane having reached sorption equilibrium was placed in the air for several hours. ATR-FTIR spectra of P(AASco -AAm)/PS membranes absorbing CO2, N2 and desorbing CO2 are shown in Fig. 3. The dry composite membrane (A), the humidified compostie membrane absorbing N2 (B) and the dry composite membrane absorbing CO2 (C) show quite similar spectra, indicating that N2 cannot react with the polymer, and without water CO2 can not react with the polymer either. The humidified composite membrane absorbing CO2 (D) shows several new bands. The bands at 831 and 1012 cm � 1 are assigned to the characteristic absorption bands of HCO3 � , the band at 1150 cm � 1 is assigned to the C-O (-COOH) stretching vibration, and the bands at 1480 and 1240 cm � 1 are the coupling of the O-H bending vibration and C-O(-COOH) stretching vibration, 30,31 which indicate that CO2 reacts with -COO � of P(AASco -AAm) in the presence of water, producing HCO3 �

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The gas permselectivity of the membranes was characterized at 25  C by a homemade apparatus 19 using CO2/N2 gas mixture (15/85 by volume). Before contacting the membrane, the feed gas and the sweep gas (H2) were both saturated with water. Gas permeance unit used in this paper is GPU (1 GPU ¼ 1 -10 � 6 cm 3 (STP) cm � 2 s � 1 cmHg � 1 ). The selectivity is the ratio of the two gas permeance. All error bars present the standard errors of performances of three membranes prepared under the same condition. In addition, it has been proven that the effect of back-diffusion of H2 on data analysis could be neglected. 19
texts.text: The gas permselectivity of the membranes was characterized at 25  C by a homemade apparatus 19 using CO2/N2 gas mixture (15/85 by volume). Before contacting the membrane, the feed gas and the sweep gas (H2) were both saturated with water. Gas permeance unit used in this paper is GPU (1 GPU ¼ 1 -10 � 6 cm 3 (STP) cm � 2 s � 1 cmHg � 1 ). The selectivity is the ratio of the two gas permeance. All error bars present the standard errors of performances of three membranes prepared under the same condition. In addition, it has been proven that the effect of back-diffusion of H2 on data analysis could be neglected. 19

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 4 shows the performance of the membrane vs. the feed pressure. As shown in Fig. 4(a), CO2 permeance decreases significantly with increasing feed pressure, which indicates that CO2 permeates through the membrane following the facilitated transport mechanism. 15-17,33 The facilitated transport is based on a reversible reaction of CO2, H2O and the carrier. With increasing feed pressure, the

With increasing feed pressure, the

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 4 Performance of the membrane as a function of the feed pressure. (a) CO2 and N2 permeance; (b) selectivity of CO2/N2. Wet coating thickness: 250 m m; drying conditions of the membrane: 30  C and 40% relative humidity; feed gas: 15 vol% CO2 + 85 vol% N2.
texts.text: Fig. 4 Performance of the membrane as a function of the feed pressure. (a) CO2 and N2 permeance; (b) selectivity of CO2/N2. Wet coating thickness: 250 m m; drying conditions of the membrane: 30  C and 40% relative humidity; feed gas: 15 vol% CO2 + 85 vol% N2.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: concentration of CO2 dissolved into the membrane increases, and thus some carriers may be tied up with some CO2 molecules and cannot combine with other CO2 any more, resulting in a decrease of CO2 permeance. 20 Unlike CO2 permeance, N2 permeance decreases slightly with increasing feed pressure, which is well consistent with the characteristic of ''solution-diffusion'' process. 34 If a gas permeates through the membrane following the ideal solution-diffusion mechanism, its permeance is independent of concentration driving force across the membrane. 34 The slight decrease of N2 permeance is due to membrane compaction which will decrease the amount of free volume and subsequently reduce the mobility of the penetrating molecules. 35 Fig. 4(b) shows that CO2/N2 selectivity increases slightly with increasing feed pressure from 0.1 to 0.5 MPa, which is also due to the membrane compaction. When the pressure exceeds 0.5 MPa, CO2/N2 selectivity decreases, which can be explained by the significantly decreased CO2 permeance and slightly decreased N2 permeance.
texts.text: concentration of CO2 dissolved into the membrane increases, and thus some carriers may be tied up with some CO2 molecules and cannot combine with other CO2 any more, resulting in a decrease of CO2 permeance. 20 Unlike CO2 permeance, N2 permeance decreases slightly with increasing feed pressure, which is well consistent with the characteristic of ''solution-diffusion'' process. 34 If a gas permeates through the membrane following the ideal solution-diffusion mechanism, its permeance is independent of concentration driving force across the membrane. 34 The slight decrease of N2 permeance is due to membrane compaction which will decrease the amount of free volume and subsequently reduce the mobility of the penetrating molecules. 35 Fig. 4(b) shows that CO2/N2 selectivity increases slightly with increasing feed pressure from 0.1 to 0.5 MPa, which is also due to the membrane compaction. When the pressure exceeds 0.5 MPa, CO2/N2 selectivity decreases, which can be explained by the significantly decreased CO2 permeance and slightly decreased N2 permeance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on the results of Fig. 3 and Fig. 4 mentioned above, we could conclude that the transport of CO2 through the wet membrane follows the facilitated transport mechanism, which is mainly attributed to the synergistic effect of the carboxylate group and the water. The facilitated transport process of CO2 through the membrane is presented in Fig. 5.
texts.text: Based on the results of Fig. 3 and Fig. 4 mentioned above, we could conclude that the transport of CO2 through the wet membrane follows the facilitated transport mechanism, which is mainly attributed to the synergistic effect of the carboxylate group and the water. The facilitated transport process of CO2 through the membrane is presented in Fig. 5.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membranes used for CO2 separation from the flue gas containing O2 21,36,37 must be antioxidative. The antioxidizability of the membrane merely with the carboxylate group as the carrier was evaluated, with the method of testing the changes in the chemical structure and the separation performance of the membrane before and after exposure to pure O2. A similar method has been employed widely to evaluate the chlorine resistance of reverse osmosis membranes, by testing the changes in the chemical structure and separation performance of the membrane before and after a short time exposure to an aqueous solution with a high concentration of free chlorine. 38,39
texts.text: The membranes used for CO2 separation from the flue gas containing O2 21,36,37 must be antioxidative. The antioxidizability of the membrane merely with the carboxylate group as the carrier was evaluated, with the method of testing the changes in the chemical structure and the separation performance of the membrane before and after exposure to pure O2. A similar method has been employed widely to evaluate the chlorine resistance of reverse osmosis membranes, by testing the changes in the chemical structure and separation performance of the membrane before and after a short time exposure to an aqueous solution with a high concentration of free chlorine. 38,39

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The chemical structure of the membrane before and after exposure to pure O2 at 0.1 MPa for about 480 h was characterized by ATRFTIR. The results are shown in Fig. 6. The spectrum of the membrane exposed to pure O2 is similar to that of the virgin membrane. It could be concluded that the membrane did not react with O2 during the testing period.
texts.text: The chemical structure of the membrane before and after exposure to pure O2 at 0.1 MPa for about 480 h was characterized by ATRFTIR. The results are shown in Fig. 6. The spectrum of the membrane exposed to pure O2 is similar to that of the virgin membrane. It could be concluded that the membrane did not react with O2 during the testing period.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Comparing with P(AASco -AAm)/PS membrane, the antioxidizability performance of polyvinylamine/PS composite membrane 40 with the amine group as the carrier was also conducted. It was found that after exposure to the air for two to three weeks, the colour of the composite membrane without any protective coating ( e.g. , polydimethylsiloxane) changed from white to light yellow. In addition, the differences in the chemical structure between the virgin membrane and the aged membrane were verified by the ATR-FTIR spectra of the two membranes. The results clearly show that after exposure to the air for some time the amine group-containing membrane without any protective coating was oxidized by O2 in the air.
texts.text: Comparing with P(AASco -AAm)/PS membrane, the antioxidizability performance of polyvinylamine/PS composite membrane 40 with the amine group as the carrier was also conducted. It was found that after exposure to the air for two to three weeks, the colour of the composite membrane without any protective coating ( e.g. , polydimethylsiloxane) changed from white to light yellow. In addition, the differences in the chemical structure between the virgin membrane and the aged membrane were verified by the ATR-FTIR spectra of the two membranes. The results clearly show that after exposure to the air for some time the amine group-containing membrane without any protective coating was oxidized by O2 in the air.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Furthermore, the energy and the total cost required for the process with P(AASco -AAm)/PS membrane were calculated with the method from our previous work. 41 For the feed rate of 30 MMSCFD (11.57 m 3 (STP) s � 1 ) (15 vol% CO2 + 85 vol% N2), the separation target of product CO2 purity > 98% and CO2 recovery > 90% can be achieved by a two-stage system with feed compressing and vacuum pumping. The membrane area required is about 1.44 -10 5 m 2 , the energy required is about 1.76 -10 3 MJpertonne CO2 recovered, and the total cost is about $65 per tonne CO 2 recovered. The results show that the total cost of this kind of membrane process is in a competitive range with the traditional chemical absorption method, since the cost and the energy consumption of the latter are about $45-80 and 3-4 -10 3 MJ per tonne CO2 recovered, 41,42 respectively.
texts.text: Furthermore, the energy and the total cost required for the process with P(AASco -AAm)/PS membrane were calculated with the method from our previous work. 41 For the feed rate of 30 MMSCFD (11.57 m 3 (STP) s � 1 ) (15 vol% CO2 + 85 vol% N2), the separation target of product CO2 purity > 98% and CO2 recovery > 90% can be achieved by a two-stage system with feed compressing and vacuum pumping. The membrane area required is about 1.44 -10 5 m 2 , the energy required is about 1.76 -10 3 MJpertonne CO2 recovered, and the total cost is about $65 per tonne CO 2 recovered. The results show that the total cost of this kind of membrane process is in a competitive range with the traditional chemical absorption method, since the cost and the energy consumption of the latter are about $45-80 and 3-4 -10 3 MJ per tonne CO2 recovered, 41,42 respectively.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It should be mentioned that in this work the carrier (carboxylate group) in the membrane was fixed only through free radical polymerization. Other methods to fix the carboxylate group in the membrane, such as interfacial polymerization, are being studied in order to obtain antioxidative membranes with higher permselectivity. Thus, the energy and total cost will be reduced dramatically, which will lead to a membrane separation more competitive and environmentally friendly. Moreover, the flue gas from the coal-fired power station usually contains fly ash and trace contaminants such as SO2 and NO2 at the same time. The fly ash can be completely removed from the flue gas after pretreatment, but some SO2 (<10 ppm) and trace NO2 still remain. 23 The membrane might suffer some damage because SO2 and NO2 can react with water to form acids. Thus, the acid resistance of the membrane is also being evaluated. The related work mentioned above will be reported in another paper.
texts.text: It should be mentioned that in this work the carrier (carboxylate group) in the membrane was fixed only through free radical polymerization. Other methods to fix the carboxylate group in the membrane, such as interfacial polymerization, are being studied in order to obtain antioxidative membranes with higher permselectivity. Thus, the energy and total cost will be reduced dramatically, which will lead to a membrane separation more competitive and environmentally friendly. Moreover, the flue gas from the coal-fired power station usually contains fly ash and trace contaminants such as SO2 and NO2 at the same time. The fly ash can be completely removed from the flue gas after pretreatment, but some SO2 (<10 ppm) and trace NO2 still remain. 23 The membrane might suffer some damage because SO2 and NO2 can react with water to form acids. Thus, the acid resistance of the membrane is also being evaluated. The related work mentioned above will be reported in another paper.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Conclusions
texts.text: Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Antioxidative composite membranes with the carboxylate group as a fixed carrier for CO2 separation from the flue gas were developed with P(AASco -AAm) as the active layer and PS ultrafiltration membrane as the substrate. The membrane was characterized using ATR-FTIR and gas permselectivity test. The results indicate that the carboxylate group fixed carrier membrane reacts reversibly with CO2 in the presence of water and the transport of CO2 through the wet membrane follows a facilitated transport mechanism.
texts.text: Antioxidative composite membranes with the carboxylate group as a fixed carrier for CO2 separation from the flue gas were developed with P(AASco -AAm) as the active layer and PS ultrafiltration membrane as the substrate. The membrane was characterized using ATR-FTIR and gas permselectivity test. The results indicate that the carboxylate group fixed carrier membrane reacts reversibly with CO2 in the presence of water and the transport of CO2 through the wet membrane follows a facilitated transport mechanism.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The ATR-FTIR spectrum of the membrane after exposure to pure O2 at 0.1 MPa for 480 h was similar to that of the virgin membrane. The separation performance of the membranes exposed to pure O2 from 152 to 344 h is equal to that of the virgin membrane. These results show that the membrane has excellent antioxidizability and a potential to be used for CO2 separation from flue gases containing oxidants.
texts.text: The ATR-FTIR spectrum of the membrane after exposure to pure O2 at 0.1 MPa for 480 h was similar to that of the virgin membrane. The separation performance of the membranes exposed to pure O2 from 152 to 344 h is equal to that of the virgin membrane. These results show that the membrane has excellent antioxidizability and a potential to be used for CO2 separation from flue gases containing oxidants.
